current_fact.fact_text: On April 16, 1941, the Italian-German Tarigo convoy’s destruction at sea was just one of many episodes in World War II’s vast, global conflict, where submarines, aircraft, and ships turned entire oceans into battlegrounds. The war accelerated technological innovation, from radar to codebreaking, that would later spill into civilian life.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

current_fact.match_reason: A world war that reshaped the planet parallels Sedna FM’s own origin episode—a kind of creative "big bang"—with a diverse, global soundtrack born from postwar cultural crossings.

queue.fact_text: On April 16, 2013, as part of the same date’s tragic tally, the Saravan earthquake underscored the importance of the moment magnitude scale, a tool seismologists use to compare the true energy released by quakes worldwide. Unlike older scales, it can reliably measure both moderate tremors and giant megaquakes on a single continuous scale.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

queue.match_reason: A precise, carefully designed scale for measuring Earth’s shudders harmonizes with an episode about paying careful, intentional attention rather than letting algorithms dictate what we feel.

queue.fact_text: On April 16, 2013, the Baga clashes in Nigeria highlighted how loosely the term "massacre" can be applied when reports are chaotic and contested. Initial accounts spoke of massive civilian death tolls and widespread burning, while later investigations tried to disentangle propaganda, panic, and on-the-ground devastation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: Conflicting narratives around a violent event fit an episode about unlearning, rethinking, and questioning the stories we accept at first glance.

queue.fact_text: On April 16, 1947, when Bernard Baruch popularized the term "Cold War," he captured a new planetary mood: a world suspended between peace and annihilation, where the loudest battles were often waged in art, music, and words. The phrase itself became a cultural lens through which decades of films, songs, and protests would be understood.

episode.title: Sedna FM #02 - Destination Sedna
episode.description: Continuing the Sedna FM journey together with family and friends! Episode two of the main series — exploring new musical territories and deepening the connection with our listeners. A collective voyage through sound.
songs: Odion Iruoje - Identify With Your Roots
Depeche mode - world in my eyes (Oil Tank Mix)

queue.match_reason: The coining of a term that defined an era of tension and global journeys through ideology complements a multi-genre episode that travels across borders and invites listeners on a shared voyage.

published.fact_text: Apollo 16, launched on April 16, 1972, was the fifth mission to land humans on the Moon and the first to target the lunar highlands. Astronauts John Young and Charles Duke explored the Descartes Highlands, while Ken Mattingly conducted experiments from lunar orbit. The mission’s geology samples reshaped scientists’ ideas about how the Moon’s crust formed.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A spaceflight milestone pairs naturally with an episode themed around "Space Shift" and reimagining emptiness as creative space, echoing the vastness of the lunar environment.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1912, aviation pioneer Harriet Quimby became the first woman to fly an airplane across the English Channel. Wearing her signature plum-colored silk flying suit, she completed the hazardous journey in about an hour, though her achievement was overshadowed in the press by news of the Titanic sinking the day before.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: Both the fact and the episode are about daring journeys across water and sky, with the sailing-trip mood mirroring Quimby’s pioneering cross-Channel flight.

published.fact_text: The Battle of Megiddo, fought around 1457 BC, is considered the first military clash recorded in detail, thanks to hieroglyphic accounts from Pharaoh Thutmose III's scribes. They documented troop movements, logistics, and even war booty, offering a surprisingly granular snapshot of Bronze Age warfare.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: An ancient battle preserved in painstaking detail aligns with a genre built on cinematic atmosphere and layered storytelling, like a sonic retelling of history’s first fully documented war.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1858, the Wernerian Natural History Society in Scotland was formally dissolved after fifty years of studying geology, zoology, and botany. Its meetings once showcased early ideas about Earth’s deep history, at a time when fossils and rock strata were starting to challenge traditional views of the planet’s age.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: A learned society devoted to nature and Earth’s rhythms sits well beside an episode built around fluid, organic jazz and reflective time by the sea.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1582, Spanish conquistador Hernando de Lerma founded the city of Salta in what is now northwestern Argentina. Nestled in the Lerma Valley, Salta would become a crossroads of Indigenous, Spanish, and later immigrant cultures, known today for its colonial architecture and Andean folk traditions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: The Spanish colonial founding of Salta connects to an exploration of Spanish musical heritage, tracing how Iberian culture traveled and transformed across continents.

published.fact_text: The Texas City disaster on April 16, 1947, began when a freighter loaded with ammonium nitrate caught fire and exploded in the port of Texas City, Texas. The blast was so powerful it created a 4.1-magnitude seismic shock, triggered additional explosions, and is still considered one of the deadliest industrial accidents in U.S. history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: The sudden, devastating explosion in Texas City echoes the high-voltage theme of an episode called "Electric Shock," channeling raw energy and impact through music instead of disaster.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1947, American financier Bernard Baruch reportedly used the phrase "Cold War" in a speech to describe the emerging tension between the United States and the Soviet Union. The term captured a new kind of conflict defined not by open battle, but by nuclear standoff, espionage, and ideological rivalry.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The era of the Cold War reshaped communication, surveillance, and technology, resonating with an episode that questions our modern, digitally mediated forms of connection and control.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 2013, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck Iran’s Sistan and Baluchestan province near the border with Pakistan. It was one of the largest earthquakes recorded in the region in decades, with tremors felt hundreds of kilometers away and buildings swaying as far as New Delhi.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A powerful earthquake and its aftermath tie into an episode about weathering storms and finding resilience when the ground beneath you suddenly shifts.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 2016, Ecuador was struck by its worst earthquake in nearly four decades, a magnitude 7.8 event along the Pacific coast. Entire neighborhoods crumpled, and yet countless stories emerged of neighbors, volunteers, and rescuers working through the night to pull survivors from the rubble.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: The long recovery from a national disaster mirrors an episode about physical healing, slow progress, and learning to rebuild after sudden rupture.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1945, the German transport ship MV Goya was torpedoed and sunk in the Baltic Sea while evacuating civilians and soldiers, killing more than 7,000 people. It ranks among the deadliest maritime disasters in history, yet remains far less known than the sinking of the Titanic.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A tragic shipwreck in cold northern waters is paired with an episode immersed in Arctic seas, trading catastrophe for contemplative encounters with marine life.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1945, U.S. forces liberated Oflag IV-C, the notorious Nazi prisoner-of-war camp housed in Colditz Castle. The fortress had been used to detain Allied officers considered high escape risks, some of whom spent years digging tunnels and constructing gliders in audacious bids for freedom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

published.match_reason: The liberation of prisoners from a fortress camp contrasts starkly with an episode about the comfort of being safely at home with friends, turning captivity into community and freedom.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1941, during World War II, British destroyers ambushed and destroyed the Italian-German Tarigo convoy off the coast of Tunisia. The night battle showed how codebreaking and radar were quietly reshaping naval warfare, allowing smaller forces to strike with uncanny precision.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: A sharp, decisive naval strike matches the fierce energy and no-permission attitude of women in rock, both stories of underdogs hitting hard against larger forces.

published.fact_text: On Orthodox Easter, April 16, 1944, Allied aircraft bombed Belgrade in an effort to disrupt German military infrastructure, causing heavy civilian casualties. The attack tragically turned a religious holiday into a day of fire and ruin for thousands of residents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: A city bombed on a sacred holiday is answered with an episode devoted to slowness and quiet, suggesting a planetary pause after the violence of the skies.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1925, the St Nedelya Church in Sofia, Bulgaria, was bombed during a funeral service in an assassination attempt by Communist militants, killing around 150 people and injuring hundreds more. It was one of the deadliest terrorist attacks in interwar Europe, shattering the sense of safety inside a sacred space.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: An attack that tore through a place of assembly contrasts with an episode celebrating powerful voices that heal, challenge, and ground communities through song.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1799, during the French Revolutionary Wars, Napoleon Bonaparte’s forces defeated Ottoman troops at the Battle of Mount Tabor in present-day Israel. Using rapid maneuver and surprise, the French infantry managed to encircle a much larger army, demonstrating the tactical speed that would later define Napoleon’s campaigns.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A mountain battlefield in the Levant connects to an episode built around mountainous landscapes and reflective travel, trading cannons for contemplative valleys.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1919, Polish forces launched the Vilna offensive, seizing Vilnius amid a tangled conflict involving Poland, Lithuania, and Soviet Russia. The city changed hands repeatedly over the following years, reflecting the shifting borders and identities of Eastern Europe after World War I.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A contested Polish campaign for territory is mirrored by a present-day journey across Poland’s landscapes, transforming lines on a map into personal roads and songs.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1961, Fidel Castro declared in a televised speech that he was a Marxist–Leninist and that Cuba would adopt communism. The announcement cemented Cuba’s alignment with the Soviet Union and set the stage for decades of Cold War showdowns just 90 miles from the United States.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: A leader publicly committing to an ideology highlights how beliefs are learned and hardened, pairing with an episode that gently questions how we form, revise, or cling to our convictions.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 1941, the Ustaše, a fascist and ultranationalist movement, took power in the newly proclaimed Independent State of Croatia under Axis protection. Their regime would become infamous for brutal persecution and genocide against Serbs, Jews, Roma, and political opponents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The rise of a fascist regime and its atrocities are juxtaposed with a fiercely political band whose music directly confronts state violence, nationalism, and systemic oppression.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 2013, Boko Haram militants clashed with government soldiers in the Nigerian town of Baga, triggering violence that left scores of civilians dead and large parts of the settlement burned. The incident highlighted how local communities often bear the brunt of insurgency and counterinsurgency alike.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Violence in a West African town resonates with an episode about desert communities whose music emerges from histories of conflict, displacement, and stubborn survival.

published.fact_text: On April 16, 2018, The New York Times and The New Yorker jointly won the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service for their investigative reporting on Harvey Weinstein’s long-hidden pattern of sexual abuse. Their articles helped ignite the global #MeToo movement, encouraging survivors worldwide to share their stories and confront abusive power.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: Investigative journalism that exposed systemic abuse aligns with an episode amplifying powerful female voices in rap, both challenging patriarchal structures and reclaiming agency.

The war accelerated technological innovation, from radar to codebreaking, that would later spill into civilian life.
